perturbation.original_text: The term “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information that is or has been received by either Party (the “ Recipient ”) from the other Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), or which the Recipient has had access to, during the four (4)-month period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), and that is either (a) marked as “confidential,” “proprietary,” or such similar term, or (b) non-public information related to the Disclosing Party’s business, which the Receiving Party should reasonably know is confidential based on the nature of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ Confidential Information ” means any and all information that is or has been received by either Party (the “ Recipient ”) from the other Party (the “ Disclosing Party ”), or which the Recipient has had access to, during the four (4)-month period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Term ”), and that is either (a) marked as “confidential,” “proprietary,” or such similar term, or (b) non-public information related to the Disclosing Party’s business, regardless of whether the Receiving Party should reasonably know is confidential based on the nature of such information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'which the Receiving Party should reasonably know is confidential based on the nature of such information', all non-public information related to the Disclosing Party’s business is now considered Confidential Information regardless of the Recipient's understanding. This creates a contradiction because previously, the Recipient needed to 'reasonably know' the information was confidential, but now, that requirement is removed, expanding the scope of what is considered Confidential Information. This impacts Section 3 on Non-Disclosure, where the Recipient's obligations are defined based on this broader definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipient shall not (a) use the Confidential Information except for the Business Purpose, or (b) disclose or make the Confidential Information available to any person or entity (other than Related Parties, as defined below) without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. Each Party shall take reasonable security precautions to protect the Confidential Information, at least as strict as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature. Without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient of Confidential Information shall restrict the disclosure and availability of Confidential Information to Recipient’s financial, tax, or legal advisors, or to its directors, officers, or employees with a demonstrable need to know such Confidential Information (each, a “ Related Party ”, and, collectively, the “ Related Parties ”), provided that any such Related Party either agrees to be bound by this Agreement or is already bound by confidentiality obligations no less restrictive than those in this Agreement. Each Party shall be responsible for the breach of this Agreement by any Related Party. Neither Party shall use any Confidential Information received by it to develop a product or service which competes with or imitates products of the Disclosing Party or engage in reverse engineering to develop similar products or services using the Confidential Information (it being acknowledged and understood that both Parties are engaged in the business of beer brewing and that competing beer products are regularly independently developed within the beer industry using the same or similar raw materials, processes and packaging). The Recipient shall prevent commingling of the Confidential Information with similar information or material of other third parties. The obligations of confidentiality and protection under this Agreement shall survive for three (3) years following the expiration of the Term.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the 'reasonably know' requirement. This directly contradicts Section 3 because it expands the scope of information the Recipient is obligated to protect, potentially including information they might not have reasonably understood to be confidential. This is a Misaligned Terminology contradiction, as the definition changes, impacting obligations elsewhere.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (a) prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party is already lawfully and rightfully known by or available to the Recipient as evidenced by prior written records; (b) through no wrongful act, fault or negligence on the part of the Recipient is or hereafter becomes part of the public domain; (c) is lawfully received by the Recipient from a third party without restriction and without breach of this agreement or any other agreement; (d) is approved for public release or use by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (e) the Recipient can demonstrate was independently developed by it without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include any information that: (a) prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party is already lawfully and rightfully known by or available to the Recipient without prior written records; (b) through no wrongful act, fault or negligence on the part of the Recipient is or hereafter becomes part of the public domain; (c) is lawfully received by the Recipient from a third party without restriction and without breach of this agreement or any other agreement; (d) is approved for public release or use by written authorization of the Disclosing Party; (e) the Recipient can demonstrate was independently developed by it without reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; or (f) is disclosed pursuant to the requirement or request of a governmental agency or court of competent jurisdiction to the extent such disclosure is required by a valid law, regulation or court order and sufficient notice is given by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party of any such requirement or request in order to permit the Disclosing Party to seek an appropriate protective order or exemption from such requirement or request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'as evidenced by prior written records' from the list of excluded information, the agreement now contradicts itself. Originally, pre-existing knowledge had to be documented to be excluded. Now, it appears oral pre-existing information is ok. This creates ambiguity about what constitutes 'lawfully and rightfully known,' particularly concerning the 'No Warranty' clause in Section 5. Without the proof of written records, it's difficult to prove the information was known before.
perturbation.contradicted_text: No Warranty. THE CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS,” AND THE DISCLOSING PARTY HEREBY DISCLAIMS ALL REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES REGARDING THE ACCURACY, SUFFICIENCY, SUITABILITY OR NON-INFRINGEMENT OF SUCH CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.justification: The removal of "as evidenced by prior written records" directly contradicts the 'No Warranty' clause. Without requiring written proof of prior knowledge, it becomes significantly harder to enforce the disclaimer of warranties related to the accuracy or sufficiency of the Confidential Information. The original text provided a mechanism (written records) to establish the baseline of knowledge, which is now absent, creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability. This aligns with the provided explanation and creates a strong contradiction making it difficult to invoke the 'No Warranty' clause effectively.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall take reasonable security precautions to protect the Confidential Information, at least as strict as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall take commercially reasonable efforts to protect the Confidential Information, with no requirement to align it with the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'reasonable security precautions, at least as strict as its own' to 'commercially reasonable efforts, with no requirement to align' weakens the protection standard. It removes the benchmark of internal security practices, now any level of commercially reasonable efforts are accepted. This is contradicted by Section 7, which allows for injunctive relief for breaches, implying a higher standard of protection than just 'commercially reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties agree that money damages would be inadequate to remedy any breach of this Agreement. As a result, a non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek, and a court of competent jurisdiction may grant, specific performance and 2 injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall be in addition to all other remedies, including money damages, available to a non-breaching party at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated a security standard 'at least as strict' as the party's own, creating a clear benchmark. The changed text weakens this by only requiring 'commercially reasonable efforts' and explicitly removing the alignment with internal standards. This creates a direct conflict with the contradicted text, which allows for injunctive relief for any breach. Injunctive relief implies a high standard of protection, which is inconsistent with the lower 'commercially reasonable efforts' standard, creating a strong contradiction. The 'misaligned terminology' perturbation definition applies because 'reasonable security precautions' and 'commercially reasonable efforts' have different implications for the required level of security, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall prevent commingling of the Confidential Information with similar information or material of other third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall attempt to prevent commingling of the Confidential Information with similar information or material of other third parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall prevent' to 'shall attempt to prevent' reduces the obligation's strength. Previously, preventing commingling was mandatory. Now, it only requires an attempt. This creates uncertainty, especially in relation to Section 6 regarding the return or destruction of Confidential Information. If commingling occurs, it becomes unclear whether the attempt was sufficient, leading to potential disputes about compliance with the return/destruction requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall prevent' to 'shall attempt to prevent' weakens the obligation to safeguard confidential information. This directly contradicts the requirement in the Contradicted Text to return or destroy 'all' tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information. If commingling occurs due to a failed attempt, it's unclear if the Recipient has fully complied with their return/destruction obligation. This creates a strong in-text contradiction regarding the recipient's responsibility and compliance with the contract.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement does not create any agency or partnership relationship between the Parties or authorize a Party to use the other Party’s name or trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement does not create any agency relationship between the Parties or authorize a Party to use the other Party’s name or trademarks.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'partnership', and only holding 'agency', there is now a contradiction within Section 4 of Non-Solicitation. While it disallows partnership, now it isn't 100% clear how to handle if one party to the agreement becomes an agency to another party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Other than through general employment listings and advertising or through the efforts of employment search firms, each Party shall not, during the Term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter, directly or indirectly, solicit or hire any employee or independent contractor of the other Party, or induce any such person to abandon their relationship with the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly disallowed both agency and partnership relationships. The changed text removes 'partnership', only disallowing 'agency', which creates a contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text discusses restrictions on soliciting or hiring employees or independent contractors. The change makes it unclear if the non-solicitation applies to parties who enter an agency relationship with the other party to the agreement since the explicit ban on partnerships has been removed. This directly impacts the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause concerning individuals who are part of an agency, creating uncertainty and a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: A prevailing Party shall be entitled to an award of its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: A prevailing Party may be entitled to an award of its attorneys’ fees and costs arising out of this Agreement, as determined by the court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled, as determined by the court' makes the award of attorney's fees discretionary rather than mandatory. This contradicts the implied expectation of full indemnification for legal expenses in Section 7 (Remedies for Breach), where injunctive relief is mentioned. The modification introduces uncertainty about whether a party can fully recover costs, even if successful in obtaining an injunction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties agree that money damages would be inadequate to remedy any breach of this Agreement. As a result, a non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek, and a court of competent jurisdiction may grant, specific performance and 2 injunctive or other equitable relief as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Such remedy shall be in addition to all other remedies, including money damages, available to a non-breaching party at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated a prevailing party 'shall be entitled' to attorney's fees, implying a mandatory award. The changed text makes the award discretionary ('may be entitled, as determined by the court'). This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that money damages may be inadequate and a non-breaching party 'shall be entitled' to seek injunctive relief. If attorney's fees are not guaranteed to be covered, the remedy of injunctive relief becomes less effective and accessible, creating a strong contradiction by potentially undermining the 'shall be entitled' aspect of seeking equitable relief. This misalignment introduces uncertainty and impacts the enforceability of remedies.
